verses.text: When the LORD shall cut off the nations, separate three cities for thee in the midst of thy land. Thou shalt prepare the way, divide the coasts of thy land into three parts, that every slayer may flee thither. Guidance: God plans the streetwork before the tragedy happens. Three cities, three regions, three runnable routes — the country itself was engineered for mercy. Justice needs infrastructure. The road to refuge must be cleared before the runner needs it.

When the LORD shall cut off the nations, separate three cities for thee in the midst of thy land. Thou shalt prepare the way, divide the coasts of thy land into three parts, that every slayer may flee thither. Guidance: God plans the streetwork before the tragedy happens. The case of the slayer which may live: whoso killeth his neighbour ignorantly, hated him not. As when a man goeth into the wood to hew wood, the axe head slippeth from the helve, lighteth upon his neighbour, that he die — he shall flee unto one of those cities, live. Lest the avenger of blood pursue while his heart is hot, overtake him because the way is long, slay him. Guidance: A neighbor swings an axe and ends a friend's life — no malice, just metal. God names the case by example, not abstraction. The mercy is concrete: the cities exist for THIS man, the road runs for HIM. Hot blood needs cool walls between it and grief.

If the LORD enlarge thy coast, give thee all the land He promised — when thou shalt keep all these commandments — then thou shalt add three cities more. Lest innocent blood be shed in thy land, and blood be upon thee. Guidance: Bigger borders need more refuges. Holiness scales with territory; mercy expands with growth. The fear named here is striking — innocent blood ON THEE. The nation that fails to protect the unintentional killer bears bloodguilt itself.

If any man hate his neighbour, lie in wait, smite him mortally, fleeth into one of these cities; the elders of his city shall send and fetch him thence, deliver him into the hand of the avenger of blood, that he may die. Thine eye shall not pity him, but thou shalt put away the guilt of innocent blood from Israel, that it may go well with thee. Guidance: The city of refuge is not a loophole. The elders of the killer's own town must investigate, and if hatred prompted the deed, they hand him out. Refuge protects accident, never ambush. Justice and mercy must both stay clear-eyed; one without the other turns ugly.

Thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee. Guidance: A stone marking the field's edge. To move it a foot in the night is to steal land slowly, in plain sight. God's law protects the boundary not because the stone is sacred but because the neighbor is. Quiet theft is still theft.

One witness shall not rise up against a man — at the mouth of two or three witnesses shall the matter be established. If a false witness testify wrong, both shall stand before the priests and the judges; if the witness be false, ye shall do unto him as he had thought to have done unto his brother. Life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot. Guidance: The lex talionis applied to LIARS. If you tried to put your brother in prison, you go to prison. If you tried to take his life, you lose yours. The penalty discourages false accusations more than any other rule. 'Eye for eye,' often misread, sets a CEILING on punishment — proportionate, never escalating.
